era_context: The mid-15th century is a period of significant geopolitical transition and cultural flourishing. In Europe, the Hundred Years' War has recently ended (1453), reshaping political alignments and allowing European powers to focus on internal consolidation and external expansion. The Ottoman Empire is expanding under Mehmed II, having conquered Constantinople in 1453. The Italian Renaissance is at its height. In East Asia, the Ming Dynasty is consolidating power after the Tumu Crisis. In Africa, the Songhai Empire is rising to prominence. In the Americas, the Aztec Empire under Montezuma I is at the height of its power.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Sub-Saharan Africa (limited written records; oral traditions not systematically documented)
Indigenous Americas (no written records in most regions; European contact documentation begins later)
Central Asia and Siberia (sparse contemporary sources)

events.title: Death of Prince Henry the Navigator of Portugal
events.description: Prince Henry the Navigator (1394–1460), the Portuguese prince who had sponsored decades of exploration along the African coast, died in November 1460. Henry had established a school of navigation at Sagres and had patronized numerous expeditions that had gradually extended Portuguese knowledge of the African coast. By the time of his death, Portuguese explorers had reached the coast of modern-day Sierra Leone. Henry's death marked the end of an era of Portuguese exploration under royal patronage, though exploration would continue under his successors. Henry's legacy was the establishment of Portugal as the leading European maritime power and the foundation for the Age of Discovery. His death represented a transition in Portuguese exploration policy, though the momentum he had created would continue.
key_figures: Prince Henry the Navigator (Infante Dom Henrique)
events.certainty_note: Prince Henry's death date is precisely documented in Portuguese records.

events.title: Battle of Bosworth Field preparations; Wars of the Roses continue
events.description: In 1460, the Wars of the Roses were continuing to reshape English politics. The conflict between the houses of York and Lancaster was intensifying, with both sides maneuvering for control of the throne. In 1460, the Yorkist forces were gaining strength, and the Lancastrian king Henry VI was increasingly isolated. The Battle of Towton would occur in 1461, but in 1460 the political and military tensions that would lead to this decisive battle were building. The Wars of the Roses represented a fundamental crisis of royal authority in England, as competing noble factions fought for control of the crown. The conflict would not be fully resolved until the Battle of Bosworth Field in 1485, but the seeds of this resolution were being sown in 1460.

sources.name: Chronicles of the Wars of the Roses

events.certainty_note: The political context of the Wars of the Roses in 1460 is well-documented in English sources.

events.title: Reign of King James III of Scotland begins
events.description: James III (r. 1460–1488) ascended to the throne of Scotland in 1460 following the death of his father James II. James III inherited the throne as a minor and his early reign was marked by regency and factional conflict among the Scottish nobility. In 1460, James III was only about 9 years old, and real power was exercised by regents and powerful nobles. His reign would be marked by efforts to strengthen royal authority and manage the powerful Scottish nobility. James III's reign represented a period of relative stability in Scotland following the death of James II, though internal conflicts would eventually lead to his deposition and death in 1488.

sources.name: Scotichronicon and other Scottish historical compilations

events.certainty_note: James III's accession date is precisely documented in Scottish records.

events.title: Zheng He's treasure voyages have ended; Ming Dynasty maritime policy established
events.description: By 1460, the great maritime voyages of Admiral Zheng He (which had ended around 1433) were definitively concluded, and the Ming Dynasty's policy of maritime withdrawal was firmly established. The treasure ships had been dismantled or left to decay, and maritime activity was increasingly restricted. The Ming Dynasty under the Yongle Emperor and his successors had shifted policy away from large-scale oceanic exploration toward continental consolidation and defense. This represented a fundamental strategic choice by the Ming court to focus resources on the Great Wall, internal administration, and land-based trade routes rather than oceanic expansion. By 1460, this policy had been in place for nearly 30 years, and it was clear that the Ming Dynasty would not resume large-scale maritime exploration.
key_figures: Ming Dynasty court officials and emperors

sources.name: Archaeological evidence of shipyards and vessel remains

events.certainty_note: The Ming policy of maritime withdrawal is well-documented in official records. By 1460, this policy had been in place for decades.

events.title: Montezuma I continues expansion of Aztec Empire; Consolidation of power
events.description: Montezuma I (r. 1440–1469), also known as Montezuma the Elder, was the fifth Aztec emperor and is remembered as one of the greatest military commanders in Aztec history. By 1460, he had been ruling for 20 years and had significantly expanded Aztec territorial control through military conquest. His campaigns extended Aztec dominion over much of central Mexico, establishing tributary relationships with numerous city-states. In 1460, Montezuma I was at the height of his power, having consolidated Aztec military and administrative structures. His reign represented the height of Aztec military power and territorial expansion before the arrival of Spanish conquistadors in the early 16th century. Montezuma I's achievements in military organization and administration set the template for Aztec imperial governance.

sources.name: Codex Mendoza (Aztec pictorial manuscript, compiled post-conquest but based on pre-conquest sources)
Oral traditions recorded by Spanish friars

events.certainty_note: Montezuma I's reign dates and major military campaigns are attested in post-conquest Aztec codices and Spanish colonial chronicles, but these sources were compiled decades or centuries after the events. The exact dating of individual campaigns carries uncertainty of ±5–10 years.

disconfirming_evidence: Prince Henry the Navigator's reputation as a visionary explorer is somewhat romanticized in popular histories. Modern scholarship emphasizes that Henry's exploration program was primarily motivated by economic gain (access to African gold and other commodities) and that the 'school of navigation' at Sagres was likely not as formal or influential as later sources suggest. The Wars of the Roses are sometimes portrayed as a conflict between clearly defined factions, but modern scholarship emphasizes the fluidity of allegiances and the role of personal ambition and local conflicts in shaping the broader civil war. Montezuma I's reign is sometimes portrayed as a 'golden age' of Aztec civilization, but recent scholarship emphasizes the militaristic and tributary nature of Aztec expansion, which created resentment among subject peoples that would later facilitate Spanish conquest.
historiographic_note: Documentation for 1460 is strong for European events (Portugal, England, Scotland) and East Asian events (China), which are recorded in contemporary archives and chronicles. The historical record is weighted toward literate, bureaucratic societies with written archives. African and American history remain underrepresented in surviving written records. The sources for this year reflect the perspectives of ruling elites and religious institutions; the experiences of common people, women, and marginalized groups are largely absent from the surviving record. For Mesoamerica, all sources are post-conquest compilations or oral traditions recorded by Spanish friars, introducing significant interpretive challenges and potential biases.

graph_edges.note: Prince Henry's death in 1460 marked the end of his direct patronage, but Portuguese exploration continued under his successors
The political tensions of 1460 led to the Battle of Towton and Edward IV's coronation in 1461
James III's accession in 1460 initiated a reign that would last until 1488
Ming maritime withdrawal contrasted with Portuguese and Spanish exploration initiatives that would dominate the Age of Discovery
Montezuma I's expansion of the Aztec Empire created the territorial and political structure that would face Spanish conquest
